observations.description: Strange black spines on both new and old cladodes. Without a doubt the tallest specimen of this species I've ever seen at roughly 9 - 10 ft tall. Areoles at the base seemed to start corking and vasculature of one of the cladodes was readily visible near the top of the plant (last photo).

observations.place_guess: Point Loma Heights, San Diego, CA, USA
observations.description:
Found a cladode of this original plant had fallen off and decided to pot it. No doubt the pot is small, but for the time being this is what I have that has good drainage! 

No idea what it is, but here I've attempted to document the growth over the months. From the initial potting, to the first growth (ended up being a fruit), to the newest three pads, I'm dumbfounded when it comes to identifying this prickly pear. I've tried using Britton and Rose's The Cactaceae to narrow it down, but clearly to no avail! Any and all help is appreciated. I'm happy to describe it further if the photos don't do it justice.

observations.place_guess: Cabrillo National Monument, San Diego, CA 92106, USA
observations.description: Strange opuntia with brownish spines instead of the more common yellow or white seen in the area. In fact, in the right light, they look like the copper colored spines of cylindropuntia prolifera. Would love to learn if this variation in color is classically accepted as within limits of the O. littoralis species or if it's some kind of hybrid. Please comment if you have expertise in identifying prickly pears!

observations.description: Majorly confusing to pin down the species. Has spines like O. oricola, but cladodes that appear quite elongated. Looking at this after the fact, it is a possibility that the cladodes to the far left on the last photo are actually a part of a other plant. Will follow up to see whether or not they're the same organism.
